narrative_blocks.header: Mount Lee — his name above the Hollywood sign
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1941 the Don Lee Broadcasting System moved its experimental TV station W6XAO to the mount of Hollywoodland — 1,700 feet above sea level. The site was chosen for elevation: combined with a 300-foot tower, it had 2,000 feet of vertical clearance, doubling the station's range from 30 to 60 miles. A swimming pool with diving board was installed at the entrance for aquatic telecasts. The ridge is still named Mount Lee — the permanent legacy of a 9th-generation Hooker descendant above one of the most recognized landmarks in America.
narrative_blocks.body: The ridge above the Hollywood sign in Los Angeles is named "Mount Lee" after Don Lee, who established his TV transmitter there in 1941.

narrative_blocks.detail: Don Lee was granted an FCC license for experimental television in 1931, beginning broadcasts of W6XAO six days a week. Engineer Harry Lubcke directed the operation; in 1932 he made history by transmitting a moving image to a receiver aboard an airplane 3,000 feet over Burbank. The station became KTSL, then KNXT, and is now KCBS-TV — the CBS flagship station for the West Coast.
narrative_blocks.body: Granted the first LA television license in 1931; his station became KCBS-TV, the CBS West Coast flagship still broadcasting today.

narrative_blocks.header: Harley Earl — the GM designer Don Lee launched
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1919 Don Lee purchased the Earl Automobile Works of Hollywood and renamed it Don Lee Coach and Body Works. He retained young Harley Earl as manager. The shop produced many custom Cadillacs for Hollywood celebrities. In 1927 Harley Earl left to become the first head of the General Motors Art and Color Section (later Styling), where he went on to design the Corvette and revolutionize American automotive aesthetics — a career launched under a Hooker descendant's roof.
narrative_blocks.body: Purchased the Earl Automobile Works in 1919 and kept Harley Earl as manager; Earl left in 1927 to become head of GM's styling department, shaping American car design for decades.

narrative_blocks.header: A television empire above the Hollywood sign
narrative_blocks.body: Heir at twenty-eight to Don Lee’s nine-million-dollar radio and Cadillac fortune, he built Los Angeles’s first television transmitter in 1931 and in 1941 raised his station atop Mt. Lee — looking down on the Hollywood sign itself.
Courts declared him mentally incompetent in August 1948; seventeen months later he fell to his death from the twelfth floor of a Wilshire Boulevard building. He lies at Mountain View in Altadena.
